2: Purpose To describe a more sustainable food sector, a supply chain approach is needed. Changing a supply chain inevitably means that various attributes of the product and its system will change. This project assumed this challenge and delivered detailed descriptions, life cycle assessment (LCA) evalua- tions, and consequence assessments of the supply chains of six commodities, i.e., milk, cheese, beef, pork, chicken, and bread, from a Swedish region. This paper presents results for the pork supply chain.
Methods In the project setup, experts on production along supply chains designed three scenarios for environmentally

improved systems. These scenarios, i.e., the ecosystem, plant nutrients, and climate scenarios, were intended to address dif- ferent clusters of environmental goals. The next step was to challenge these scenarios by considering their possible conse- quences for products and systems from the food safety, sen- sory quality, animal welfare, consumer appreciation, and (for primary production only) cost perspectives. This led to chang- es in production system design to prevent negative conse- quences. The final supply chains were quantified using LCA and were again assessed from the three perspectives.

Results and discussion The scenario design approach worked well, thoroughly and credibly describing the production sys- items. Assessment of consequences bolstered the credibility and quality of the systems and results. The LCA of pig pro- duction and smoked ham identified large potentials for im- provement by implementing available knowledge: global warming potential (GWP) could be reduced 21-54 % and marine eutrophication by 1445% The main reason for these improvements was improved productivity (approaching the best producers' current performance), though dedicated mea- sures were also important, resulting in increased nitrogen ef- ficiency, more varied crop rotations for crop production and better production management, and improved animal health and manure management for animal production. Reduced post-farm wastage contributed as did reduced emissions from fertilizer production.

Conclusions The working approach applied was successful in integrating LCA research with food system production exper- tise to deliver results relevant to supply chain decision- makers. The consequence assessments brought considerable value to the project, giving its results greater credibility. By introducing constraints in the form of "no negative
consequences and no increased costs," the work was "guided" SO that the scenario design avoided being hampered by too many opportunities.
Keywords Consequence assessment Environment Food system scenarios . Future production LCA Sustainable food chains

6: Designing more sustainable food production systems entails looking at the whole supply chain, from primary production and its inputs to consumers. This is obvious to the life cycle assessment (LCA) community and is being embraced by a growing segment of society. LCA research and case studies are critical for understanding the impact profile of products and systems. Most studies analyzing and proposing more sus- tainable food production systems use an aggregated approach to identify improvement potentials in various life cycle steps, based on separate assessments of each step. This is a useful approach and may work as a "semi-backcasting exercise," as the results can provide a starting point for focusing research and development on how to implement improvements. However, such an approach is less valuable when seeking practical information on relevant actions to be taken by food chain actors (e.g., farmers, industry, retailers, consumers, and various public decision-makers).

To move LCA research closer toward hands-on decision- making, novel approaches are needed. Designing environ- mentally improved supply chains at a more detailed level than is usually accomplished by LCA calls for new working methods that combine the systems' perspective from LCA with deeper and more detailed knowledge of production systems.

Another crucial aspect of food systems is the broad range of food-related constraints, such as food safety, food quality, and animal welfare. Consumer reactions are also critical when new solutions are introduced in the food supply chain, and overall, economic viability is a condition to be met. Changing food supply chains inevitably means changing a range of attributes of the product and its system, and neglecting these attributes in the quest for more sustainable solutions will seriously hamper stakeholder interest and delay or stop necessary improvements in environmental performance.

The basic intention of the project was to determine how much the environmental impact of food production could be reduced if we combined available solutions from different parts of the chain to form new, better functioning supply chains, provided that no negative consequences arise. The aim was to present solutions for specific food supply chains offering improved environmental performance, solutions that

are realistic and concrete enough for food chain stakeholders to use in their decision-making. The focus was on improved production in the short term changes in consumption patterns being beyond the scope of the project. No radical changes were considered, as the aim was to quantify potentials based on current knowledge and technologies. A second aim was to develop and apply an integrated working process involving various disciplines and synthesizing their knowledge.

The project's objectives were to (1) describe in detail the supply chains of six commodities (i.e., milk, cheese, beef, pork, chicken, and bread) from the Västra Götaland Region in Sweden in 2012; (2) describe future scenarios for these supply chains, assuming the same commodity production vol- ume as in 2012, while improving environmental performance and maintaining or improving key attributes mentioned above (e.g., product safety and quality); (3) using LCA, quantify the environmental impact of these supply chains, using the current situation as the reference point; and (4) quantify the costs of primary production in future scenarios and the reference sce- nario, based on current cost levels.

This paper describes part of the project, namely, the supply chain for smoked, sliced ham. While several LCA studies of pig production have been published (e.g., Basset-Mens and van der Werf 2005; Basset-Mens et al. 2007; Halberg et al. 2010, and studies mentioned below), we found no integrated, wide-ranging study of pork products or production. For exam- ple, Audsley et al. (2009) addressed environmental improve- ment considerations but used a top-down approach involving little interaction with production experts and applying no constraints, such as preventing negative consequences. Strid Eriksson et al. (2005) and Garcia-Launay et al. (2014) both analyzed opportunities to improve pig production but focused only on changing feed composition. Stern et al. (2005) devel- oped future scenarios for Swedish pig production covering only primary production, though the scenarios encompassed a broad spectrum of objectives. Accordingly, the study pre- sented here can be seen as a continuation of the study by Stern et al. (2005).
The objective of this paper is to present the scenario design process and method, the resulting scenarios as such, and, fi- nally, the LCA evaluation of the scenarios.

11: Scenarios have long been used to manage uncertain futures and support decision-making (Schnaars 1987) in all kinds of settings and for various purposes. A vast literature treats sce- nario development, covering theory as well as practical imple- mentation. Generally, scenarios are descriptions of plausible futures outlining how the present becomes the future

(Schnaars 1987). A further definition is that scenarios can be distinguished along three dimensions: (1) explorative (fore- casting)-normative (anticipatory, backcasting), (2) quantita- tive-qualitative, and (3) medium term-long term (Meyer 2007; Schnaars 1987). Scenarios differ from forecasting in that they incorporate important narrative elements, i.e., quali- tative descriptions of the drivers and causes of the future states that often constitute them. Depending on the goal of the sce- nario study, various scenario development methodologies should be employed (Schnaars 1987).

Our approach builds mainly on that described by Millet (1988, as cited by Bunn and Salo 1993), in which scenario development has two elements: (1) describing possible future environments in which the studied system will work and (2) describing strategic options for these environments. In line with the first element, the present study refers to "possible future environments" for "goal scenarios" describing the var- ious environmental goals that the system should meet. The second element, "strategic options for these environments," is here called "solution scenarios," as they entail finding solu- tions that best meet the requirements set in the goal scenarios. Scenario analysis can have three objectives: (1) developing a range of forecasts to aid in selecting strategies, (2) testing a selected strategy against various futures, and (3) studying sev- eral hypothetical futures to advance managerial learning (Bunn and Salo 1993). Our objectives fit the second objective, although our intention was to develop a strategy rather than test a predetermined one. We apply a normative approach. Normative scenarios are generally based on descriptions of the drivers and causes of the future states (Bunn and Salo 1993). In our case, we believe that the need for environmental improvements itself explains the future states (i.e., goal sce- narios) defined.
12: Scenarios have long been used to manage uncertain futures and support decision-making (Schnaars 1987) in all kinds of settings and for various purposes. A vast literature treats sce- nario development, covering theory as well as practical imple- mentation. Generally, scenarios are descriptions of plausible futures outlining how the present becomes the future

(Schnaars 1987). A further definition is that scenarios can be distinguished along three dimensions: (1) explorative (fore- casting)-normative (anticipatory, backcasting), (2) quantita- tive-qualitative, and (3) medium term-long term (Meyer 2007; Schnaars 1987). Scenarios differ from forecasting in that they incorporate important narrative elements, i.e., quali- tative descriptions of the drivers and causes of the future states that often constitute them. Depending on the goal of the sce- nario study, various scenario development methodologies should be employed (Schnaars 1987).

13: First, the environmental impacts generally acknowledged as the most critical were identified using the literature (Rockström et al. 2009; SEPA 2013-Sweden's official envi- ronmental goals). The next step was to match these environ- mental impacts against the full list of LCA impact categories defined in ReCiPe (Goedkoop et al. 2009) and identify impact categories in which food production plays an important role, doing SO using published LCAs. Based on this, a priority list of impact categories was formulated, the highest priority being given to impacts to which food systems contribute substan- tially and that were identified as globally and nationally im- portant (Table 1).

A categorization was conducted in which impact categories corresponding to environmental objectives that, when food systems are assessed, rarely conflict with each other were clustered in the same group. Furthermore, based on a large number of LCA studies, impact categories corresponding to

Table 1 List of midpoint impact categories in ReCiPe, prioritization based on (1) global importance of the category and (2) how important food systems are to the impact categories (project internal assessment)

Priority identified|Impact categories included in the priority group 1|Marine eutrophication, freshwater eutrophication, freshwater ecotoxicity, biodiversity, climate change, agricultural land occupation, and mineral consumption 2|Terrestrial acidification, terrestrial ecotoxicity, natural land transformation, fossil fuel consumption, water consumption, ozone depletion, human toxicity, and photochemical ozone formation 3|Particulates, urban land occupation, marine ecotoxicity, and radiation

potentially conflicting goals were put in different groups. This exercise resulted in three goal scenarios, presented in Table 2. Thereafter, solution scenarios were designed in ways that best satisfied the goal scenarios. Several measures were applicable in more than one solution scenario; such overlap is not a flaw in the method as the measures are accounted for only once in each solution scenario. Goal scenarios support decision- making in cases in which certain measures might lead to con- flict between environmental goals but are still interesting for addressing one of the goals (e.g., reducing greenhouse gas emissions by reducing tillage, which could lead to increased pesticide use). In the post-farm chain, it became evident that most improvement measures were aligned with scenario three, climate, SO only one solution scenario per product was devel- oped for this part of the supply chain.

15: In the project setup, experts on production along the supply chain (e.g., agronomists, animal scientists, food engineers, and packaging and supply chain management experts) de- signed a reference production system (in the following text referred to as the reference scenario or system) reflecting the present system and based on 2012 data. The reference system represents the average production in 2012. There are ap- proaches to making the inventory more efficient and accurate by using actual production data from farm traceability systems (Bellon-Maurel et al. 2015). Such an approach was impossible here, as the available systems did not contain sufficiently ac- curate data, and the aim was to describe the entire production in the region rather than production on individual farms. Then, environmentally improved systems for each product chain were developed by the production experts using the literature and their own expertise combined with expertise on the envi- ronmental impact of food supply chains and their various at- tributes and consequences (product safety and quality, animal welfare, production costs, and consumer aspects). solution scenarios consisted partly not only of technological and management solutions, but also of quantifications of pro- duction performance. Especially for animal production, few concrete technologies were cited; instead, production poten- tials were quantified and possible measures to realize these potentials were discussed. One solution scenario was designed for each goal scenario (described above). The close coopera- tion between food chain experts and with experts on conse- quences ensured overall improved supply chains and solution scenarios. Finally, the environmental impact of the reference and solution scenarios for the supply chains was quantified using LCA and cost calculations (for the agricultural portion only). The general working process is described in Fig. 1. The time frame for implementing improvements was 5 to 10 years, SO designing the solution scenarios entailed gather- ing available knowledge of functioning supply chains. The primary production part included the entire animal and/or crop production system (i.e., the beef, dairy, pig, chicken, and bread grain production systems) in the specified region, while be- yond primary production and primary processing (e.g., slaughter and milling), specific products were studied (i.e., smoked ham, frozen chicken fillets, sirloin steak, aged cheese, drinking milk, and bread). The later stages of the chain were limited to just single products because of the existence of a

huge diversity of products in later stages: for example, while a farmer delivers just one product, i.e., the slaughter pig, the meat processor delivers hundreds of products through various distribution channels. Of course, the farming system is very complex, with numerous inputs and connections between its parts, but because its products are much fewer, it is possible to follow the total flows. This approach meant that we delivered LCA results for the entire primary production of the region studied, but only results for single products for the chain be- yond primary production, except in the case of dairy, in which both milk and cheese were studied. The products were select- ed using criteria such as market volume and potential chal- lenges to one or more of the attributes mentioned above. The approach also included interactions between the agri- cultural systems. Hence, the crop production part of the chains, in which each production system produced an impor- tant part of the feed required, and the manure management part, in which the manure was to be used on the farm, func- tioned as the shared space for this interaction. Feed can be, and often is, produced and traded between farms. The total need for feed, quantified by animal nutrition experts with a view to high production efficiency and low environmental impact of feed raw materials corresponding to the various goal scenari- os, determined the design of crop rotations for the various
Fig. 1 Principal description of the working process in the project; shaded boxes indicate areas not treated in this article

types of farms. As a starting point, possible rations were assessed using emission factors from a feed database devel- oped at The Swedish Institute for Food and Biotechnology (SIK). In addition, wheat for bread production and surplus feed crops could also be produced by the farms if it improved the crop rotations. Through discussions, the feed ration for- mulations were adjusted to facilitate improved crop produc- tion, including manure management, in order to find efficient compromises between optimal feeding and optimal crop pro- duction systems. Throughout the process, the work was guid- ed by food LCA experts who supported the decision-making, ensuring the collection of relevant data in an appropriate for- mat and facilitating discussions between expert groups.

The next step was to challenge the proposed scenarios by considering their possible consequences for products and sys- tems from the food safety, sensory quality, animal welfare, consumer appreciation, and primary production cost perspec- tives. All scenarios were assessed, mainly qualitatively, and modified using an iterative process to take all perspectives into account at the same time in the final supply chains.
The finalized supply chains were quantified by LCA, using cost calculations for the primary production part of the chain, and were assessed for the possible consequences mentioned above.

The functional units of primary production were the total production volumes for 2012 and 1 kg of packaged product of the specific type chosen at the point of retail reception. The results comprised both single-product LCAs and region-wide environmental impacts and resource uses for agriculture and primary processing, including by-product management.
2.3.1 Process of and interactions in developing the solution scenarios
The proper functioning of all interactions between the expert areas described above was critical to project success. Between

expert groups, several interactions differing in formality and frequency occurred throughout the project. The four main expert groups involved in the project addressed project man- agement, production systems, LCA, and consequences. In ad- dition, a reference group connected to the project consisted of food supply chain stakeholders, i.e., national federations (farmers, food industries, and retailers), academia, and indi- vidual businesses from food industry. The project groups also contained sub-groups treating, for example, crop production, animal production, and processing. The project groups were distributed throughout Sweden; few of their members had previously worked together, which put extra demands on in- teraction management. The role of project management was to facilitate interaction and, more importantly, to manage and transform the information and decisions created in the inter- actions, to ensure overall project progress. Most intra-project interactions occurred via web-based meetings, though physi- cal meetings were also held. Reference group meetings as well as the first project meeting were thoroughly prepared, whereas internal project meetings were less formal and much more frequent, i.e., every 1-2 weeks during intense periods (the process is described in Fig. 2). Following the process shown in Fig. 2, the solution scenarios were evaluated and the results were presented and discussed with the reference group.

Interaction with the reference group incorporated ele- ments of Delphi technique (Rowe et al. 1991), but there was no member anonymity and no formal questionnaires were used. Feedback on material developed in preceding internal project steps was requested before reference group meetings and was discussed at the ensuing meetings.

The products were select- ed using criteria such as market volume and potential chal- lenges to one or more of the attributes mentioned above. The approach also included interactions between the agri- cultural systems. Hence, the crop production part of the chains, in which each production system produced an impor- tant part of the feed required, and the manure management part, in which the manure was to be used on the farm, func- tioned as the shared space for this interaction. Feed can be, and often is, produced and traded between farms. The total need for feed, quantified by animal nutrition experts with a view to high production efficiency and low environmental impact of feed raw materials corresponding to the various goal scenari- os, determined the design of crop rotations for the various
Fig. 1 Principal description of the working process in the project; shaded boxes indicate areas not treated in this article

types of farms. As a starting point, possible rations were assessed using emission factors from a feed database devel- oped at The Swedish Institute for Food and Biotechnology (SIK). In addition, wheat for bread production and surplus feed crops could also be produced by the farms if it improved the crop rotations. Through discussions, the feed ration for- mulations were adjusted to facilitate improved crop produc- tion, including manure management, in order to find efficient compromises between optimal feeding and optimal crop pro- duction systems. Throughout the process, the work was guid- ed by food LCA experts who supported the decision-making, ensuring the collection of relevant data in an appropriate for- mat and facilitating discussions between expert groups.

The next step was to challenge the proposed scenarios by considering their possible consequences for products and sys- tems from the food safety, sensory quality, animal welfare, consumer appreciation, and primary production cost perspec- tives. All scenarios were assessed, mainly qualitatively, and modified using an iterative process to take all perspectives into account at the same time in the final supply chains.
The finalized supply chains were quantified by LCA, using cost calculations for the primary production part of the chain, and were assessed for the possible consequences mentioned above.

The functional units of primary production were the total production volumes for 2012 and 1 kg of packaged product of the specific type chosen at the point of retail reception. The results comprised both single-product LCAs and region-wide environmental impacts and resource uses for agriculture and primary processing, including by-product management.
2.3.1 Process of and interactions in developing the solution scenarios
The proper functioning of all interactions between the expert areas described above was critical to project success. Between

expert groups, several interactions differing in formality and frequency occurred throughout the project. The four main expert groups involved in the project addressed project man- agement, production systems, LCA, and consequences. In ad- dition, a reference group connected to the project consisted of food supply chain stakeholders, i.e., national federations (farmers, food industries, and retailers), academia, and indi- vidual businesses from food industry. The project groups also contained sub-groups treating, for example, crop production, animal production, and processing. The project groups were distributed throughout Sweden; few of their members had previously worked together, which put extra demands on in- teraction management. The role of project management was to facilitate interaction and, more importantly, to manage and transform the information and decisions created in the inter- actions, to ensure overall project progress. Most intra-project interactions occurred via web-based meetings, though physi- cal meetings were also held. Reference group meetings as well as the first project meeting were thoroughly prepared, whereas internal project meetings were less formal and much more frequent, i.e., every 1-2 weeks during intense periods (the process is described in Fig. 2). Following the process shown in Fig. 2, the solution scenarios were evaluated and the results were presented and discussed with the reference group.

Interaction with the reference group incorporated ele- ments of Delphi technique (Rowe et al. 1991), but there was no member anonymity and no formal questionnaires were used. Feedback on material developed in preceding internal project steps was requested before reference group meetings and was discussed at the ensuing meetings.

Following the process shown in Fig. 2, the solution scenarios were evaluated and the results were presented and discussed with the reference group.

Interaction with the reference group incorporated ele- ments of Delphi technique (Rowe et al. 1991), but there was no member anonymity and no formal questionnaires were used. Feedback on material developed in preceding internal project steps was requested before reference group meetings and was discussed at the ensuing meetings.
23: The life cycle stages included in the study are primary production (including inflows of inputs such as
Fig. 2 Overview of project interactions; PM project management, PROD production experts, LCALCA experts, CONS consequence experts, and REF reference group

fertilizers and pesticides), processing, production of packaging material, and all transport. The downstream delimitation is the product delivery at point of retail reception. LCA calculations were performed using SimaPro software (PRé Consultants 2007), and back- ground data were from the EcoInvent 3.0 database. The following environmental impact categories were considered using impact assessment methods suggested in the ILCD Handbook (European Commission 2011): global warming potential (GWP); terrestrial, freshwater, and marine eutrophication; and acidification. Cumulative energy demand (CED) was quantified according to Frischknecht and Jungbluth (2003). The use of virgin phosphorous was estimated by quantifying the inflow of phosphorus in feed and fertilizer, as no other inflows were relevant. Finally, for pesticides, we used a simple method that quantifies the number of recommended doses of all pesticides as an indicator of pesticide de- pendency in the cropping system.

Allocation was based mainly on the economic value of the products, according to EcoInvent 3. Allocation between single products from the carcass was based on internal company prices, which is usually not done. This resulted in the absolute results per kilogram of meat not being directly comparable to those of previous studies. For allocation within crop rotations, the emis- sions and resource use that occur were charged to the actual crop, except for measures benefitting all crops, such as phosphorus application and green manuring, which were distributed to all crops on an area basis. Emissions from manure in animal housing and storage were included in the animal system, whereas emissions from manure transport and spreading were allocated to crop production.

26: Using the goal scenarios, solutions for each part of the supply chain were developed that, in combination, are called solution scenarios. In pig production, the most important aspects were feed efficiency, feed composition, animal production efficiency, and manure management. In the post-farm chain, the possible im- provements were smaller but included less wastage in industry and distribution, reduced energy use, and shifting to bioenergy. Furthermore, optimized transport and packaging could be of in- terest. In the project, all details were described and quantified; however, due to space limitations, we present only the most prom- inent changes for each scenario. Below, the solution scenarios for the pork supply chain are presented, using the reference scenario as a basis. We should keep in mind that solutions should be implementable within a 10-year time frame and that radical chang- es were not considered. To quantify practical production results (i.e., weight gain and feed use per pig), data on the best- performing current farmers were used as a starting point, with minor adjustments based on the qualitative scenario descriptions. Detailed descriptions of all solution scenarios are found in Göransson et al. (2014) and Stenberg et al. (2014).

As the functional unit in this case was the production vol- ume of pig meat in the studied Västra Götaland Region in 2012, the following changes and consequences were intro- duced in the animal production in all solution scenarios:

<list>1. More piglets per SOW and lower recruitment of SOWS lead to fewer SOWS needed (based on current best-performing producers); 
2. A higher meat percentage leads to fewer fattening pigs for the same total meat volume, SO fewer SOWS and piglets are needed (extrapolating previous breeding achievements, combined with expert judgments); 
3. Less feed use per fattening pig and more piglets per SOW, with just a small increase in SOW feed, lead to a reduced overall feed need (based on current best-performing producers); 
4. Lower pig mortality leads to fewer SOWS needed (based on current best-performing producers); and 
5. Less nitrogen and phosphorous are present in manure due to higher feed efficiency with the use of synthetic amino acids and phytase for increased phosphorous uptake (based on expert judgments and research results).</list>

Together, these changes led to a significantly reduced need for feed and, hence, for arable land, and less plant nutrients in the manure. This had consequences for the crop production part of the system (below) and also reduced resource use as such.

31: <header>3.1.2 Solution scenarios for feed crop production </header>
The crop production system is connected to all animal pro- duction systems in the project (i.e., dairy, poultry, beef, and

pork production systems) as well as to bread production. Crop production farms produce feed crops used for all animal types, and animal farms can produce surplus feed crops used at other farms. Our approach was to define the farm type for the var- ious production systems, i.e., dairy, beef, pork, and poultry husbandry and crop farming. The studied region has two main types of production areas, i.e., plain districts with large fields and intensive agriculture and forest districts with smaller, scattered fields and slightly more extensive agriculture. Reflecting the current situation, we assumed that the poultry, pork, and crop production farming occurred in the intensively farmed region and the beef production in the more extensively farmed region. Dairy farming was assumed to occur in both regions, but mainly in the more extensive region.

Below, only the results for farms producing both pig feed and pigs are presented, a choice made due to space limitations. Actually, some pig feed raw materials come from other crop- producing farms or are imported into the region. The main crop production measures concern crop rotations and the use of manure and other plant nutrient sources. Reduced pesticide use was important in the ecosystem solution scenario and in farm management options to support biodiversity, such as features as hedges that break up monotonous landscapes. A portion (2.5 of arable in the plain area was set aside to support biodiversity. Generally, varied crop rotations, with leys and other non-cereal crops, reduce the need for plant protection, facilitate mechanical weeding, and reduce the need for mineral fertilizers. Catch crops were introduced as much as possible to reduce nutrient losses, and clover/grass leys were grown for green manure. In the plant nutrients and climate solution scenarios, the same crop rotation, farm management, and plant protection measures were used. In the plant nutrients scenario, precision fertilization was applied. This similarity was because both efficient nutrient management and reduced climate impact required similar solutions. Table 4 presents the results for the feed crop production part of the pork supply chain.

33: Improved manure management requires solutions for storing and treating as well as optimizing manure use in crop rota- tions. In the reference scenario, manure was stored in lagoons covered with a natural crust and spread on grain crops, mainly in autumn. The ecosystem scenario used essentially the same handling system as did the reference scenario, changing only the spreading application rates. Ammonia and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions were drastically reduced in the plant nutri- ents scenario by tightly covering the storage lagoon and acid- ifying the slurry during spreading. Solutions for the climate scenario included anaerobic digestion of the manure to pro- duce biogas, which in turn increased the amount of plant- available nitrogen and reduced losses to the environment;

furthermore, the digestate was acidified to prevent ammonia losses during storage and spreading. Generally, measures to prevent nitrogen losses also reduce the need for mineral fer- tilizers. For manure application, the solution scenarios restrict- ed doses SO as to equal crop requirements, although buffer doses of phosphorus could be applied for the next year's crops. Application timing was also identified as a feasible measure to reduce losses and was included in the solution scenarios. Generally, more manure was spread in spring be- fore sowing and in early summer for growing crops; autumn spreading was avoided as much as possible.

35: <header>3.1.4 Solution scenarios for the post-farm supply chain </header>

This part of the supply chain was divided into four parts: processing, distribution, packaging, and by-product manage- ment. Some measures are needed to be coordinated to avoid sub-optimization. In the post-farm supply chain, few improve- ment measures involved conflicts between goal scenarios; one solution scenario was therefore developed, and the data used are presented in Table 5.

For processing, it was concluded that major improvements in energy use often require new investments and possibly new ways of producing. Considering the short time frame applied in the project, we focused on incremental changes. The poten- tial energy savings in slaughter and meat processing are con- sidered to be in the 20-30% range, as a food industry average.

The current system is relatively well optimized in terms of logistics, offering limited room for improvement. However, some minor improvements were identified, such as more fuel-efficient trucks, more fuel-efficient driving, as well as more volume-efficient packaging (see below), leading to fuel savings in the order of 4 %. Furthermore, the use of biodiesel was introduced in the climate scenario to reduce GHG emissions.

For packaging, it was necessary to consider the function (i.e., product protection to maintain quality and prevent wast- age), space efficiency for efficient distribution, and, finally, the environmental impact of the packaging material itself. In the reference scenario, the smoked and sliced ham was pack- aged in a plastic tray (package size, 180 g) and a modified atmosphere was used. In the solution scenario, so-called wal- let packaging was used instead, using the same modified at- mosphere. The benefit was reduced packaging material vol- ume per kilogram of product while maintaining the shelf life; this increased transport efficiency while using less secondary packaging.

Wastage of raw materials and finished products constitutes a large share of post-farm environmental impacts, and its causes are often interconnected with processing, distribution, storage, and packaging. On average, 3 % of products are wasted in the Swedish food industry (excluding retail and households), but variation is great both between and within
Table 5 Solution scenarios for the post-farm supply chain for the three goal scenarios developed

|Reference-situation in 2012|Solution scenarios Improved logistics||4 % reduction in fuel use Wastage in the supply chain|5.5%|3.2% Carcass meat percentage, slaughtered pigs|58.2|60.2 Energy use in processing||25 % reduction Electricity used|Average Swedish mix|Fossil free Fuel used for vehicles|Fossil|Biodiesel Fuel used for heat in processing|Swedish district heating mix|Biofuel (woodchips) ²
a These measures were used only in the climate scenario

companies (Lindbom et al. 2013). Moreover, the food supply chain is a "push system" with a short time frame, meaning that primary production cannot adjust its production volumes on short notice-for example, pigs keep growing regardless of whether there is demand for pork products in the coming week-which adds to the complexity. Based on a detailed analysis of possible measures to reduce food industry wastage (Lindbom et al. 2013), we found that wastage in ham produc- tion was 5.5%, mainly from slicing. The expert judgment was that this wastage could be reduced by 40%.

Food processing, especially meat processing, generates large volumes of by-products. Only approximately 35 % of the live weight of a slaughtered pig ends up as meat and processed meat products. The opportunities to use these large flows of by-products are restricted by the European Directive on Animal By-products (European Commission 2002), regu- lating the disposal and use of animal by-products not intended for human consumption and focusing entirely on safety issues. Due to regulations and certification systems in Sweden, the by-product handling will not be changed in the solution sce- narios. For low-risk by-products (85 % of total volume), plant nutrients and climate solution scenarios assume anaerobic di- gestion and the use of residual slurry as fertilizer on farmland, justifying the zero allocation to meat for waste management.

Furthermore, the use of biodiesel was introduced in the climate scenario to reduce GHG emissions.

For packaging, it was necessary to consider the function (i.e., product protection to maintain quality and prevent wast- age), space efficiency for efficient distribution, and, finally, the environmental impact of the packaging material itself. In the reference scenario, the smoked and sliced ham was pack- aged in a plastic tray (package size, 180 g) and a modified atmosphere was used. In the solution scenario, so-called wal- let packaging was used instead, using the same modified at- mosphere. The benefit was reduced packaging material vol- ume per kilogram of product while maintaining the shelf life; this increased transport efficiency while using less secondary packaging.

Wastage of raw materials and finished products constitutes a large share of post-farm environmental impacts, and its causes are often interconnected with processing, distribution, storage, and packaging. On average, 3 % of products are wasted in the Swedish food industry (excluding retail and households), but variation is great both between and within

39: <header>3.2 Assessment of consequences of solution scenarios </header>

As the project assesses hypothetical systems, it was impossi- ble to use experimentally based methods, SO the analyses of consequences were theoretical and qualitative studies based on available knowledge. The consequences included in this part of the project concerned product safety (microbiological), animal welfare, product sensory qualities, and consumer as- pects. Throughout the project, the design of the solution sce- narios was scrutinized by consequence analysis experts who gave advice on which solutions were favorable and which ones should be avoided. Finally, a report on each area of consequence was compiled; these are presented by Sonesson et al. (2014).

For product sensory quality, the impacts of several aspects were assessed, i.e., feed, breed, and possible castration of male pigs; stress during live animal transport, reception, and waiting at the slaughterhouse; and slaughter. For the pig meat, impacts from processing, recipes, packaging, and distribution were in- cluded. Findings included that medical rather than surgical cas- tration is preferred as it incurs less risk of boar taint and a lower fat content, which is considered positive for sensory quality.

Microbiological food safety was approached using Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP) to identify critical points and corrective measures, while predictive modeling was used to calculate bacterial concentrations dur- ing processing and storage. Six processing lines for pork and smoked sliced ham were evaluated: ordinary slaughter and deboning of pork, automated slaughter, and the processing and storage of smoked ham. Some critical changes were found between the reference and solution scenarios. The identified effects on microbial safety and quality can be controlled using relevant control measures. The optimization of heat process- ing must be related to the microbial quality of the raw material and to the extent and likelihood of post-processing contamination.

As animal housing did not change between scenarios, an- imal health and welfare was considered to be little affected in the solution scenarios compared with the reference scenario. However, as less straw was used for dry SOWS in all solution scenarios and this might negatively affect animal welfare, bet- ter knowledge among animal keepers and more strategic use of straw were suggested.

The cost of primary production is obviously an important consequence of changed production systems. The cost aspect was included in discussions of choices of farm size, animal husbandry system (e.g., no outdoor pig production), possible feeds, and crop rotation (these need to deliver decent yields over the cycle). The cost analysis demonstrated that the solu- tion scenarios have lower production costs per kilogram of live weight pig, resulting mainly from decreased feed costs (both lower volumes and lower cost per ton) and increased productivity in animal production, i.e., more products deliv- ered with the same working hours and capital investment.

42: The environmental impacts of the scenarios were quan- tified mainly using LCA, but other simpler measures were used for some aspects (see Sect. 2). Figure 3 shows the GWP and acidification potential in the sce- narios. There were large potentials for GWP reductions in all solution scenarios largely due to the increased productivity of pig production, leading to an overall 17 % reduction in feed volumes and lower impact per kilogram of feed produced due to improvements in the crop production system. On average, the impact per ki- logram of grain was reduced by 25 % in the ecosystem and plant nutrients scenarios and by 40 % in the climate scenario, mainly due to the introduction of low-emission nitrogen fertilizers (climate), more efficient use of nitro- gen overall in the production system, and increased yields. The increased use of synthetic amino acids added to the reduction by lowering the need for protein feeds such as soy- or rapeseed meal. Methane emissions from manure management were reduced by covering the manure lagoons in the plant nutrients scenario and by anaerobic digestion in the climate scenario. The reduc- tion of processing wastage and the increased meat per- centage on carcasses added a 5 % reduction overall.

Acidifying emissions were dominated by ammonia from manure management (i.e., storing and spreading). Increased nitrogen efficiency in crop production led to a general reduction of acidifying emissions in all scenari- os. Increased efficiency of pig production also led to a reduced amount of nitrogen in the manure (synthetic amino acids allow for lower total nitrogen content in the feed and hence also in manure). The reduction can be said to be dramatic in the plant nutrients scenario, in which manure lagoons are tightly covered and the

manure is acidified at spreading. The reduction shown for "Feed" in Fig. 3 was due to manure acidification at spreading and more productive crop production, mainly via higher yields. The production of plastic for packag- ing also contributed to the impact and was reduced in all solution scenarios due to less packaging being used per kilogram of ham.

Another general improvement was the reduced wastage and higher meat yield, which reduced emissions at "the bot- tom line," i.e., each percentage point less waste or increased production translates into a similar improvement in environ- mental performance.

The marine eutrophication potential is presented in Fig. 4. This impact was dominated by nitrate leaching from arable land. As for the previously presented impact categories, there were general improvements stemming largely from less need for feed, reduced wastage, and increased meat yield. Emissions from crop production were also reduced by prop- erly timed manure spreading, precision application of mineral fertilizers, and generally higher nitrogen efficiency.

Finally, Fig. 4, showing pesticide use, indicates that the ecosystem scenario performed much better for all farm types than did either the reference or the two other solution scenar- ios, because reducing pesticide use was an important goal when designing the ecosystem scenario. Several measures were introduced in the crop production system, such as me- chanical weeding, more frequent ploughing, green manuring, and a general restraint in using pesticides. In the plant nutri- ents scenario, most of the same measures were applied but no mechanical weeding or green manuring was conducted, SO the pesticide use was higher than in ecosystem scenario but lower than in the reference and climate scenarios. The climate sce- nario used reduced tillage and direct drilling, but as the crop rotations were better than in the reference scenario, the pesti- cide use increased little despite the reduced tillage.
Fig. 3 Greenhouse gas emissions (kg CO2-equiv./kg ham) and potential acidification in the scenarios (Mol H -equiv./kg ham)
Fig. 4 Pesticide use (hectare-doses/ha) and potential marine eutrophication (kg N-equiv./kg ham) for the scenarios

The increased use of synthetic amino acids added to the reduction by lowering the need for protein feeds such as soy- or rapeseed meal. Methane emissions from manure management were reduced by covering the manure lagoons in the plant nutrients scenario and by anaerobic digestion in the climate scenario. The reduc- tion of processing wastage and the increased meat per- centage on carcasses added a 5 % reduction overall.

Acidifying emissions were dominated by ammonia from manure management (i.e., storing and spreading). Increased nitrogen efficiency in crop production led to a general reduction of acidifying emissions in all scenari- os. Increased efficiency of pig production also led to a reduced amount of nitrogen in the manure (synthetic amino acids allow for lower total nitrogen content in the feed and hence also in manure). The reduction can be said to be dramatic in the plant nutrients scenario, in which manure lagoons are tightly covered and the

manure is acidified at spreading. The reduction shown for "Feed" in Fig. 3 was due to manure acidification at spreading and more productive crop production, mainly via higher yields. The production of plastic for packag- ing also contributed to the impact and was reduced in all solution scenarios due to less packaging being used per kilogram of ham.

Another general improvement was the reduced wastage and higher meat yield, which reduced emissions at "the bot- tom line," i.e., each percentage point less waste or increased production translates into a similar improvement in environ- mental performance.

The marine eutrophication potential is presented in Fig. 4. This impact was dominated by nitrate leaching from arable land. As for the previously presented impact categories, there were general improvements stemming largely from less need for feed, reduced wastage, and increased meat yield. Emissions from crop production were also reduced by prop- erly timed manure spreading, precision application of mineral fertilizers, and generally higher nitrogen efficiency.

Finally, Fig. 4, showing pesticide use, indicates that the ecosystem scenario performed much better for all farm types than did either the reference or the two other solution scenar- ios, because reducing pesticide use was an important goal when designing the ecosystem scenario. Several measures were introduced in the crop production system, such as me- chanical weeding, more frequent ploughing, green manuring, and a general restraint in using pesticides. In the plant nutri- ents scenario, most of the same measures were applied but no mechanical weeding or green manuring was conducted, SO the pesticide use was higher than in ecosystem scenario but lower than in the reference and climate scenarios. The climate sce- nario used reduced tillage and direct drilling, but as the crop rotations were better than in the reference scenario, the pesti- cide use increased little despite the reduced tillage.

47: The overall approach in the project, integrating expert compe- tence from various fields of food and agricultural sciences, was extremely beneficial in terms of developing the solution scenarios. By combining expertise on production systems with LCA competence, detailed production systems that maintained the system's perspective of LCA could be de- signed. The involvement of experts on the consequences of products and systems supplied valuable insights into how the design affected, for example, food safety and animal welfare, and into what measures could be suggested without jeopardiz- ing these critical constraints. The broad range of involved competences also enhanced the credibility of the results among stakeholders, as the project group as a whole really understood the limitations of and opportunities for food pro- duction in a broad sense. Working in such an integrated and synthesizing manner is a novel approach in our field, and it brought about needs for new ways of working and interacting between researchers in different fields. Efficient communica- tion and organization of the project proved to be crucial, as was the mindset of individuals in the project group. The sce- nario design process was not completely structured, and it could have been more efficient if, for example, Delphi tech- niques had been employed.

Few critical consequences were identified, partly due to the short time horizon applied in the project and partly because close interaction between researchers eliminated questionable solutions at an early stage. If the time horizon had been longer and the project had included radical changes, the role of consequence as- sessments would have been more prominent.
The intention was to avoid value-based choices in the project. This was partly accomplished by the structured

method used, in which the use of goal scenarios was central, but the iterative working process also contribut- ed. It was impossible to be fully objective, however, as some qualitative choices needed to be made. Our insis- tence on full transparency regarding all choices made throughout the project does not resolve this issue but facilitates useful discussion of the results.

The trade-off between "reasonable" and optimized so- lutions is also an issue. On one hand, stakeholder rele- vance in the short term calls for known solutions; on the other hand, substantial improvements often call for more radical changes. The use of constraints on conse- quences guided the discussions efficiently; however, the definition and choice of constraints were crucial to the outcome of the study. We applied relatively strict con- straints, SO several promising solutions were omitted. The economic assessment included only costs, dismissing all solutions that increased cost, regardless of the better prices that environmentally better products may obtain and of possibly increased subsidies. The use of constraints served well in making the project con- crete, and it would be interesting to apply the method- ology using looser constraints allowing more radical changes in the production systems.

Finally, a broad approach generally implies new chal- lenges to researchers and project management. With the involvement of a large number of highly qualified ex- perts, each with limited overview of the entire project, clearly defined goals in each sub-project were vital. For the success of the project, it was critical to maintain focus and interest among all project members. Therefore, during the more intensive parts of the pro- ject, frequent but short meetings between each sub- group and the project management were scheduled to maintain the pace and keep the information flow effi- cient-not least importantly, in both directions.

52: The LCA results indicated large potentials to reduce the envi- ronmental impact of producing smoked ham-indeed, of pro- ducing all types of pork product-because a large share of the improvements occurred in the agricultural portion of the life cycle. The main driver of the improvement potentials was increased productivity in both crop and animal production. Strid Eriksson et al. (2005) found a 15 % reduction in GHG emissions, and Garcia-Launay et al. (2014) found a 26° % de- crease with the use of synthetic amino acids. However, none of these studies considered any improvements in either crop production or increased animal efficiency. It could be argued that our systems compromise animal welfare, for example, but in our study, improved animal health is a prerequisite for achieving these production results, according to the consulted veterinarian expertise. Achieving higher production efficiency requires better livestock surveillance and better competence among animal keepers. The production data used for the so- lution scenarios are credible and reasonable, as most of these data reflect what the best-performing actors deliver today.

At the same time, it must be said that the results are based on what could be called a utopian view, i.e., that all actors and individuals will do everything right, at all times and at all places along the supply chain. To achieve the improved envi- ronmental performance presented here, important investments in production and management competence are needed. The increased production efficiency and lowered costs also require larger production units, which might be detrimental to social sustainability, especially in the countryside, where agriculture is often important for local economies and social networks.

We included improvement measures along the entire supply chain, despite an impressive list of evidence from the literature that the upstream processes, i.e., primary production, are the most important ones for environmental impacts in food production systems. The results indicate that these minor improvements, tak- en together, actually contributed substantially in some impact categories. This is important, not the least for communication and for raising awareness among stakeholders.

The results also clearly indicate that there are conflicts be- tween environmental goals in the agricultural sector. The most prominent one is the conflict between reduced pesticide use and more "technical" impacts, such as global warming and acidifica- tion, which might explain the sometimes heated debate on or- ganic versus conventional production. The conflict is a matter of what sustainability goals that the discussants prioritize.

It must also be emphasized that the solution scenarios are not ready-made suggestions for stakeholders to implement, but extreme examples that could very well be combined. By designing goal scenarios for groups of environmental goals, a very useful platform for further studies and discussions was created, and it was also possible to introduce broader sustain- ability goals such as social aspects or farm income.

55:
<list>The working approach applied in the project was success- ful in integrating LCA research with food system produc- tion expertise in order to deliver results that are relevant to decision-makers in the supply chain. 
The consequence assessments brought considerable value to the project, efficiently guiding the design of solution scenarios and eliminating in advance potential solutions that were unrealistic and counterproductive. The conse- quence assessments also gave the results greater credibil- ity among stakeholders in the supply chain. 
By introducing constraints in the form of "no negative consequences and no increased costs," the project was "guided" SO that the scenario design could avoid being hampered by too many opportunities, which is often the case when future systems are designed. 
The issue of value-based choices is important and needs to be transparently managed and communicated. 
There are large potentials to improve environmental per- formance in the food sector by applying known technolo- gy and management measures, but doing SO requires large investments in both competence and capital.</list>
